severity_reason: same-MMSI conflict (two vessels broadcasting one identity)

explanation.method: AIS position-anomaly analysis (impossible-speed jumps and same-instant conflicts)
drivers: 34 physically impossible movements for one MMSI
fastest implied speed 54,534 kn (a vessel cannot move this fast)
positions span 49 nm, including 1 same-instant conflicts (two vessels, one MMSI)

caveats: Suspected AIS spoofing or MMSI conflict, not proven and not necessarily illegal.
Could also be a faulty GPS/transceiver or a transcription error in the feed.
Flagged only on a sustained pattern (several anomalies), not a single bad fix.
An inspection lead: confirm the vessel identity against authoritative records.

explanation.method: GFW two-vessel encounters (Miller et al. 2018, DOI 10.3389/fmars.2018.00240)
drivers: fishing vessel (AU) met a carrier (VU) for 26 h
held 39 m apart at 0.0 kn (consistent with transshipment)
two-vessel at-sea encounter (GFW / Miller et al. 2018)

caveats: Apparent at-sea transshipment encounter, not proven and not necessarily illegal.
Two AIS vessels held close and slow; some encounters are legitimate (bunkering, aid).
Source: GFW two-vessel encounters (Miller et al. 2018); an inspection lead.
GFW data is CC BY-NC; only derived in-EEZ dossiers are shown here, with attribution.
